label: Edwin Hubble
description: American astronomer (1889–1953)

label: Galileo Galilei
description: Italian polymath (1564–1642)

label: Nicolaus Copernicus
description: Polish mathematician and astronomer (1473–1543)

label: Giordano Bruno
description: Italian Dominican friar, philosopher and mathematician (1548–1600)

label: Tycho Brahe
description: Danish astronomer and alchemist, 1546–1601

label: Christiaan Huygens
description: Dutch mathematician, physicist and astronomer (1629–1695)

label: William Herschel
description: German-born British astronomer and composer (1738–1822)

label: Mikhail Lomonosov
description: Russian polymath (1711-1765)

label: Svante August Arrhenius
description: Swedish astronomer, chemist and physicist (1859–1927)

label: Aryabhata
description: Indian mathematician-astronomer

label: Jabir ibn Hayyan
description: 8th-century Islamic alchemist and author

label: Thomas Young
description: English polymath (1773-1829)

label: Hipparchus
description: 2nd century Greek astronomer, geographer and mathematician

label: Ulugh Beg
description: Timurid sultan, astronomer and mathematician (1394–1449)

label: Harold Urey
description: American physical chemist

label: Nasir al-Din al-Tusi
description: Persian astronomer (1201–1274)

label: Caroline Herschel
description: German-British astronomer (1750-1848)

label: Neil Armstrong
description: American astronaut and lunar explorer (1930–2012)

label: Yuri Gagarin
description: Soviet pilot and cosmonaut, first human in space (1934–1968)

label: Valentina Tereshkova
description: Russian cosmonaut and pilot, first woman to have flown in space

label: Mars
description: Fourth planet from the Sun, known as the Red Planet

label: Jupiter
description: Fifth planet from the Sun and largest planet in the Solar System

label: Saturn
description: Sixth planet from the Sun, famous for its extensive ring system

label: Moon
description: Earth's only natural satellite

label: Sun
description: The star at the center of our Solar System

label: Earth
description: The third planet from the Sun and home to humanity

label: Mercury
description: Smallest planet and closest to the Sun

label: Venus
description: Second planet from the Sun, hottest planet in the Solar System

label: Uranus
description: Seventh planet from the Sun, rotates on its side

label: Neptune
description: Eighth and farthest planet from the Sun

label: Pluto
description: Dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt, formerly considered a major planet

label: Io
description: Innermost Galilean moon of Jupiter, most volcanically active body

label: Europa
description: Moon of Jupiter with a subsurface ocean beneath ice shell

label: Ganymede
description: Largest moon in the Solar System, orbits Jupiter

label: Callisto
description: Second-largest moon of Jupiter with ancient, cratered surface

label: Titan
description: Largest moon of Saturn with dense atmosphere and liquid methane lakes

label: Enceladus
description: Moon of Saturn with water geysers erupting from south polar region

label: Mimas
description: Small moon of Saturn, distinctive for its large impact crater

label: Iapetus
description: Moon of Saturn with distinctive two-toned coloring and equatorial ridge

label: Dione
description: Moon of Saturn with wispy terrain features

label: Triton
description: Largest moon of Neptune, orbits retrograde

label: Charon
description: Largest moon of Pluto, forms binary system with Pluto

label: Phobos
description: Larger and inner moon of Mars

label: Deimos
description: Smaller and outer moon of Mars

label: Ariel
description: Moon of Uranus with extensive system of canyons and valleys

label: Umbriel
description: Moon of Uranus with dark surface and prominent crater

label: Titania
description: Largest moon of Uranus with vast canyons

label: Oberon
description: Second-largest moon of Uranus

label: Miranda
description: Moon of Uranus with heavily cratered and fractured surface

label: Rhea
description: Second-largest moon of Saturn

label: Tethys
description: Moon of Saturn with massive canyon Odysseus Chasma

label: Hyperion
description: Moon of Saturn with chaotic rotation

label: Phoebe
description: Moon of Saturn with retrograde orbit and dark surface

label: Janus
description: Moon of Saturn that shares orbit with Epimetheus

label: Proxima Centauri
description: Closest known star to the Sun at 4.24 light-years

label: Sirius
description: Brightest star in the night sky, in the constellation Canis Major

label: Betelgeuse
description: Red supergiant star in Orion constellation, likely to explode as supernova

label: Rigel
description: Blue supergiant star in Orion constellation

label: Aldebaran
description: Orange giant star, brightest star in Taurus constellation

label: Altair
description: Bright white star in Aquila constellation, part of Summer Triangle

label: Deneb
description: Blue-white supergiant in Cygnus constellation, part of Summer Triangle

label: Polaris
description: North Star, brightest star in Ursa Minor, used for navigation

label: Antares
description: Red supergiant star in Scorpius constellation

label: Canopus
description: Second brightest star in the night sky, in Carina constellation

label: Milky Way
description: Our galaxy, spiral galaxy containing our Solar System

label: Andromeda Galaxy
description: Nearest large galaxy to the Milky Way, about 2.5 million light-years away

label: Large Magellanic Cloud
description: Satellite galaxy of the Milky Way, visible from Southern Hemisphere

label: Small Magellanic Cloud
description: Smaller satellite galaxy of the Milky Way

label: Orion Nebula
description: Bright nebula in Orion constellation, visible to the naked eye

label: Crab Nebula
description: Supernova remnant in Taurus, result of 1054 supernova explosion

label: Ring Nebula
description: Planetary nebula in Lyra constellation shaped like a ring

label: Apollo 11
description: First crewed mission to land on the Moon in 1969

label: Apollo 13
description: Lunar mission famous for the phrase 'Houston, we've had a problem'

label: Voyager 1
description: Space probe launched in 1977, now in interstellar space

label: Voyager 2
description: Space probe launched in 1977, visited multiple outer planets

label: Mars Rover Curiosity
description: NASA rover exploring Mars's surface since 2012

label: New Horizons
description: Space probe that flew by Pluto in 2015 and continues to explore the Kuiper Belt

label: Hubble Space Telescope
description: Space telescope that has revolutionized astronomy since 1990

label: International Space Station
description: Orbital research facility crewed by international astronauts

label: Buzz Aldrin
description: Astronaut, lunar module pilot on Apollo 11

label: Big Bang
description: Cosmological model for the origin of the universe

label: asteroid
description: minor planet of the inner Solar System; not a comet

label: Uranus
description: seventh planet in the Solar System, mainly composed of hydrogen and helium

label: terrestrial planet
description: planet that is composed primarily of silicate rocks or metals. Within the Solar System, the terrestrial planets are the inner planets closest to the Sun, i.e. Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars

label: nebular hypothesis
description: astronomical theory that the Solar System formed from nebulous material

label: constellation
description: image on the celestial sphere consisting of stars according to any current of historical system or description

label: Ceres
description: dwarf planet in the Solar System and largest asteroid of the main asteroid belt

label: dwarf planet
description: planetary-mass object in hydrostatic equilibrium which is not a satellite of another one, but which has still not significantly cleared its neighborhood to dominate it gravitationally and maintain its cohesion

label: natural satellite
description: celestial body that orbits a planet or other smaller body in turn directly orbiting a star

label: Ursa Major
description: constellation visible throughout the year in most of the northern hemisphere

label: Orion
description: constellation straddling the celestial equator

label: Eris
description: dwarf planet in the Solar System

label: Titan
description: largest moon of Saturn

label: Pleiades
description: open cluster in the constellation of Taurus

label: Europa
description: Galilean moon of Jupiter

label: Kuiper Belt
description: area of the Solar System beyond the planetary orbits comprising small bodies

label: Apollo 11
description: first Moon landing and fifth crewed flight of the United States Apollo program

name: James Webb Space Telescope
notes: Next-generation space telescope launched 2021, observes in infrared, successor to Hubble, already producing revolutionary images of early universe and exoplanet atmospheres

name: Cassini-Huygens
notes: NASA/ESA mission to Saturn (1997-2017), extensively studied Saturn's rings, moons, and atmosphere; Huygens probe landed on Titan

name: Mars Perseverance Rover
notes: NASA Mars rover launched 2020, searching for signs of ancient microbial life, collecting rock samples, carrying Ingenuity helicopter

name: Mars Opportunity Rover
notes: NASA rover that operated on Mars from 2004-2019, discovered evidence of ancient liquid water, far exceeded planned 90-day mission

name: Mars Spirit Rover
notes: NASA Mars rover (2004-2010), twin of Opportunity, explored Gusev Crater, found evidence of past water activity

name: Pioneer 10
notes: First spacecraft to travel through the asteroid belt and past Jupiter (1972), carried the famous Pioneer plaque with a message for extraterrestrials

name: Pioneer 11
notes: First spacecraft to visit Saturn (1979), followed Pioneer 10 through the asteroid belt and past Jupiter

name: Juno spacecraft
notes: NASA mission orbiting Jupiter since 2016, studying Jupiter's atmosphere, magnetic field, and interior structure in detail

name: Dawn spacecraft
notes: NASA mission (2007-2018) that orbited both Vesta and Ceres in the asteroid belt, first spacecraft to orbit two extraterrestrial bodies

name: Parker Solar Probe
notes: NASA probe launched 2018 to study the Sun's outer atmosphere (corona), the closest any spacecraft has come to the Sun

name: OSIRIS-REx
notes: NASA mission that collected samples from asteroid Bennu and returned them to Earth in 2023; first US asteroid sample return

name: Rosetta spacecraft
notes: ESA mission (2004-2016) that orbited comet 67P and deployed Philae lander, first mission to orbit and land on a comet

name: Chandrayaan-1
notes: India's first lunar mission (2008-2009), discovered water molecules on the Moon, major milestone for ISRO

name: Chang'e 4
notes: Chinese mission (2019) that made first-ever soft landing on the far side of the Moon, deployed Yutu-2 rover

name: Hayabusa2
notes: JAXA mission that collected samples from asteroid Ryugu and returned them to Earth in 2020, second asteroid sample return

name: Viking 1
notes: First successful Mars lander (1976), searched for signs of life and studied Martian weather and soil composition

name: Mars Pathfinder
notes: NASA lander (1997) that deployed Sojourner rover, first Mars rover mission, demonstrated airbag landing technique

name: MESSENGER
notes: First spacecraft to orbit Mercury (2011-2015), mapped entire planet, discovered water ice in permanently shadowed craters

name: Magellan spacecraft
notes: NASA mission (1989-1994) that mapped 98% of Venus surface using radar, revealing vast volcanic landscape

name: Galileo spacecraft
notes: NASA mission (1989-2003) that orbited Jupiter for 8 years, discovered evidence of subsurface ocean on Europa, deployed probe into Jupiter's atmosphere

name: Chandra X-ray Observatory
notes: NASA space telescope launched 1999 to observe X-ray sources, named after Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar

name: Spitzer Space Telescope
notes: NASA infrared space telescope (2003-2020), observed distant galaxies, star-forming regions, and exoplanet atmospheres

name: Kepler space telescope
notes: NASA telescope (2009-2018) that discovered over 2,600 confirmed exoplanets by detecting tiny dips in starlight

name: Very Large Telescope
notes: ESO facility in Chile with four 8.2m telescopes that can work as interferometer, among the world's most powerful optical instruments

name: Atacama Large Millimeter Array
notes: Radio telescope array in Chile's Atacama Desert, 66 antennas working together to observe cold dust and gas in space

name: Arecibo Observatory
notes: Former 305-meter radio telescope in Puerto Rico (1963-2020), used for SETI, radar astronomy, and sent the famous Arecibo Message to space

name: FAST telescope
notes: Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical Telescope in China, world's largest single-dish radio telescope since 2016

name: LIGO
notes: Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory; made first direct detection of gravitational waves in 2015 from merging black holes

name: Vera C. Rubin Observatory
notes: Observatory under construction in Chile named after Vera Rubin, will conduct Legacy Survey of Space and Time covering the entire southern sky every few nights

name: black hole
notes: Region of spacetime where gravity is so strong nothing can escape, not even light; predicted by general relativity and first imaged in 2019

name: neutron star
notes: Incredibly dense remnant of massive star after supernova, packs more than one solar mass into a sphere ~20 km across

name: pulsar
notes: Highly magnetized rotating neutron star emitting beams of electromagnetic radiation; first discovered by Jocelyn Bell Burnell in 1967

name: quasar
notes: Extremely luminous active galactic nucleus powered by supermassive black holes, among the brightest objects in the universe

name: supernova
notes: Powerful stellar explosion that can briefly outshine an entire galaxy; source of heavy elements heavier than iron in the universe

name: gamma-ray burst
notes: Most energetic electromagnetic events in the universe, thought to occur during supernova explosions or neutron star mergers

name: dark matter
notes: Hypothetical invisible matter making up ~27% of the universe; inferred from gravitational effects on visible matter and light bending

name: dark energy
notes: Mysterious energy making up ~68% of the universe, responsible for accelerating expansion of the universe; discovered in 1998

name: cosmic microwave background
notes: Electromagnetic radiation filling the universe, relic from ~380,000 years after the Big Bang; key evidence for Big Bang cosmology

name: gravitational waves
notes: Ripples in spacetime caused by accelerating massive objects; predicted by Einstein in 1915, first directly detected by LIGO in 2015

name: solar flare
notes: Sudden intense burst of radiation from the Sun's surface, can disrupt radio communications and cause auroras on Earth

name: aurora borealis
notes: Natural light display in Earth's sky near polar regions, caused by charged particles from the Sun interacting with the atmosphere

name: comet
notes: Icy small Solar System body that develops a coma and tail when near the Sun; historically seen as omens

name: Halley's Comet
notes: Most famous periodic comet, visible from Earth every ~75-76 years; last perihelion 1986, next in 2061

name: meteor shower
notes: Celestial event where Earth passes through debris trail of a comet, creating streaks of light; famous examples include Perseids and Leonids

name: Oort Cloud
notes: Hypothetical shell of icy bodies at the outermost reaches of the Solar System, source of long-period comets

name: exoplanet
notes: Planet orbiting a star other than the Sun; over 5,500 confirmed as of 2024, many discovered by Kepler telescope

name: Solar System
notes: The Sun and all objects gravitationally bound to it, including 8 planets, dwarf planets, moons, asteroids, and comets

name: light-year
notes: Distance light travels in one year (~9.46 trillion km or 5.88 trillion miles); used to measure astronomical distances

name: Vega
notes: Fifth brightest star in the sky, in Lyra constellation, part of Summer Triangle; was the North Star ~14,000 years ago

name: Spica
notes: Brightest star in Virgo constellation, binary star system about 250 light-years away

name: Regulus
notes: Brightest star in Leo constellation, one of the brightest stars in the night sky; spins so fast it's oblate-shaped

name: Fomalhaut
notes: Brightest star in Piscis Austrinus, ~25 light-years away; surrounded by a prominent debris disk and the first star imaged with a suspected exoplanet

name: Alpha Centauri
notes: Nearest star system to the Sun at 4.37 light-years; a triple system including Proxima Centauri, Alpha Centauri A and B

name: Arcturus
notes: Brightest star in Bootes constellation and northern celestial hemisphere, orange giant about 37 light-years away

name: Castor
notes: Second-brightest star in Gemini, actually a sextuple star system consisting of three binary pairs

name: Pollux
notes: Brightest star in Gemini constellation, an orange giant about 34 light-years away with a confirmed exoplanet

name: white dwarf
notes: Dense stellar remnant after a low/medium mass star expels its outer layers; Earth-sized but very massive, will eventually cool into a black dwarf

name: red giant
notes: Late stage of stellar evolution for low/medium mass stars, where the outer layers expand enormously; the Sun will become a red giant in ~5 billion years

name: Eagle Nebula
notes: Young open cluster and emission nebula 7,000 light-years away, famous for the 'Pillars of Creation' image taken by Hubble in 1995

name: Horsehead Nebula
notes: Dark nebula in Orion constellation shaped like a horse's head, one of the most identifiable nebulae in the night sky

name: Pillars of Creation
notes: Famous Hubble photograph showing elephant trunk pillars of interstellar gas in the Eagle Nebula, stellar nurseries forming new stars

name: Sombrero Galaxy
notes: Spiral galaxy 31 million light-years away in Virgo, famous for its bright nucleus, large bulge, and prominent dust lane resembling a sombrero

name: Whirlpool Galaxy
notes: Interacting grand-design spiral galaxy about 23 million light-years away, first galaxy to have its spiral structure identified

name: Triangulum Galaxy
notes: Third-largest galaxy in the Local Group after Andromeda and Milky Way, about 3 million light-years away, most distant object visible to naked eye

name: Cassiopeia A
notes: Supernova remnant in Cassiopeia about 11,000 light-years away, brightest extrasolar radio source in the sky, remnant of supernova ~1680

name: Sagittarius A*
notes: Supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way, 4 million solar masses; imaged by Event Horizon Telescope in 2022

name: Cassiopeia constellation
notes: W-shaped constellation in the northern sky, named after mythological Ethiopian queen, contains several interesting star clusters and nebulae

name: Scorpius constellation
notes: Prominent zodiac constellation in the southern sky, contains Antares (red supergiant), resembles a scorpion with a curving tail

name: Leo constellation
notes: Zodiac constellation representing a lion, contains Regulus (brightest star) and several bright galaxies; source of Leonid meteor shower

name: Gemini constellation
notes: Zodiac constellation representing twins Castor and Pollux; source of Geminid meteor shower, one of the most active of the year

name: Taurus constellation
notes: Zodiac constellation containing Aldebaran, Pleiades star cluster, Hyades cluster, and the Crab Nebula supernova remnant

name: Sagittarius constellation
notes: Zodiac constellation near the center of the Milky Way, contains the teapot asterism, numerous nebulae and star clusters

name: Aquarius constellation
notes: Zodiac constellation representing water-bearer, contains the Helix Nebula (closest planetary nebula to Earth)

name: Ursa Minor constellation
notes: Constellation containing Polaris (North Star) at the tip of its tail, used for navigation for centuries

name: Haumea
notes: Dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt with an unusually elongated shape from rapid rotation, has two moons (Hi'iaka and Namaka) and a ring

name: Makemake
notes: Dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt, third-largest known dwarf planet, named after Rapa Nui creation deity

name: Johannes Kepler
notes: German astronomer (1571-1630) who formulated the three laws of planetary motion, foundational to understanding orbits

name: Carl Sagan
notes: American astronomer (1934-1996) and science communicator, authored Cosmos series, advocated for SETI, helped design Pioneer plaque and Voyager Golden Record

name: Neil deGrasse Tyson
notes: American astrophysicist and popular science communicator, director of Hayden Planetarium, hosted the revived Cosmos series

name: Vera Rubin
notes: American astronomer (1928-2016) who provided key observational evidence for dark matter through galaxy rotation curves

name: Jocelyn Bell Burnell
notes: British astrophysicist who discovered pulsars in 1967 as a graduate student; the Nobel Prize for the discovery controversially went to her supervisors

name: Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin
notes: British-American astronomer (1900-1979) who discovered that stars are mainly composed of hydrogen and helium, a foundational discovery in astrophysics

name: Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar
notes: Indian-American astrophysicist (1910-1995) who calculated the Chandrasekhar limit for white dwarf collapse, Nobel Prize 1983; Chandra X-ray Observatory named after him

name: Annie Jump Cannon
notes: American astronomer (1863-1941) who created the Harvard spectral classification system for stars (OBAFGKM), classified over 350,000 stellar spectra

name: Henrietta Swan Leavitt
notes: American astronomer (1868-1921) who discovered the period-luminosity relationship for Cepheid variable stars, enabling measurement of cosmic distances

name: Sputnik 1
notes: First artificial satellite to orbit Earth (1957), launched by Soviet Union, signaled the start of the Space Age and Space Race

name: Apollo program
notes: NASA crewed spaceflight program (1961-1972) that landed 12 astronauts on the Moon in 6 missions, greatest feat of human space exploration

name: Space Shuttle
notes: NASA's partially reusable low Earth orbit spacecraft (1981-2011), flew 135 missions, launched Hubble and built ISS; Challenger and Columbia disasters

name: Mir space station
notes: Soviet/Russian space station (1986-2001), first modular space station, hosted over 100 cosmonauts, precursor to ISS

name: Vostok 1
notes: Soviet spacecraft that carried Yuri Gagarin on the first human spaceflight on April 12, 1961, completing one orbit of Earth

name: Mariner 4
notes: First spacecraft to provide close-up images of Mars (1965), revealed a heavily cratered, apparently lifeless surface, transforming views of Mars

name: general relativity
notes: Einstein's theory of gravitation (1915) describing gravity as curvature of spacetime; predicts black holes, gravitational waves, and expansion of the universe

name: Hubble's law
notes: Observation that galaxies recede from Earth at a speed proportional to their distance, demonstrating the expanding universe; discovered by Hubble in 1929

name: Voyager Golden Record
notes: Gold-plated copper disk attached to both Voyager probes containing sounds and images representing life on Earth, meant for potential extraterrestrial finders

name: Event Horizon Telescope
notes: Global network of radio telescopes that in 2019 produced first image of a black hole (M87*) and in 2022 imaged Sagittarius A*

name: asteroid belt
notes: Region between Mars and Jupiter containing millions of irregularly shaped rocky objects; Ceres is the largest body

name: Vesta asteroid
notes: Second-largest object in the asteroid belt, orbited by Dawn spacecraft 2011-2012; differentiated body with an iron core

name: Bennu asteroid
notes: Near-Earth carbonaceous asteroid visited by OSIRIS-REx; samples returned to Earth 2023, considered a potentially hazardous asteroid

name: Ryugu asteroid
notes: Near-Earth asteroid visited by Hayabusa2; samples returned to Earth 2020 contained organic molecules and amino acid precursors

name: Apophis asteroid
notes: Near-Earth asteroid briefly considered a potential Earth impact risk in 2029 before refined calculations ruled it out; will make close flyby

name: Io volcanic activity
notes: Io, Jupiter's innermost Galilean moon, is the most volcanically active body in the Solar System due to tidal heating from Jupiter's gravity

name: Europa Clipper
notes: NASA mission launched 2024 to study Europa's subsurface ocean and assess habitability potential; multiple flybys of Europa around Jupiter

name: TRAPPIST-1
notes: Ultra-cool dwarf star 39 light-years away with 7 Earth-sized exoplanets, 3 in the habitable zone; most planetary system favorable for life search

name: Proxima Centauri b
notes: Exoplanet orbiting Proxima Centauri in the habitable zone, closest known exoplanet to Earth at ~4.24 light-years

name: heliocentrism
notes: Astronomical model placing the Sun at the center of the Solar System; championed by Copernicus and later proven correct by Galileo and Kepler

name: parallax
notes: Apparent shift of an object against a distant background when viewed from different positions; used to measure distances to nearby stars

name: Cepheid variable
notes: Type of pulsating star with a precise period-luminosity relationship used as 'standard candles' for measuring cosmic distances

name: M87 galaxy
notes: Giant elliptical galaxy in Virgo cluster, 53 million light-years away; contains the first ever imaged black hole (M87*) captured in 2019

name: Kepler-452b
notes: Exoplanet discovered by Kepler telescope in 2015, sometimes called 'Earth's cousin', orbits a Sun-like star in the habitable zone

name: solar wind
notes: Stream of charged particles released from the upper atmosphere of the Sun; shapes the magnetospheres of planets and creates cometary tails

name: magnetosphere
notes: Region of space around a planet dominated by its magnetic field; protects Earth from solar wind and cosmic rays

name: Van Allen radiation belts
notes: Zones of energetic charged particles held in place by Earth's magnetic field; discovered in 1958 by Explorer 1, named after James Van Allen

name: Lagrange point
notes: Position in space where gravitational forces balance; L2 Lagrange point is where JWST is parked, 1.5 million km from Earth

name: Voyager interstellar space
notes: Heliosphere boundary (heliopause) where Voyager 1 became first human-made object to enter interstellar space in 2012
